does: This paper identifies and characterizes trust boundary confusion in vision-language agentic systems, where agents must distinguish between legitimate environmental signals (like traffic lights) and adversarially crafted visual injections designed to manipulate behavior. The authors construct a dual-intent dataset and evaluation framework to systematically test whether seven different LVLM-based agents can reliably balance responding to genuine environmental cues while rejecting misleading ones. Their evaluations across multiple embodied settings reveal that current agents consistently fail this trade-off, either blindly following harmful visual injections or ignoring legitimate signals. The paper demonstrates vulnerabilities under both structure-based and noise-based attack patterns and proposes a multi-agent defense strategy to mitigate these failure modes.
connects: This work extends research on adversarial robustness and visual security for embodied AI systems, building on prior work examining adversarial examples against vision models and prompt injection attacks on language models. It combines insights from vision security literature with agent safety research, addressing how perception vulnerabilities propagate through decision-making in autonomous systems. The paper responds to growing concerns about LVLM-based agents deployed in real-world environments where both legitimate environmental signals and potential adversarial manipulations coexist, a challenge not adequately addressed by prior work on either clean robustness or simple adversarial perturbations. It also connects to broader research on agent alignment and out-of-distribution detection in embodied AI.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more robust perception-to-action pipelines in embodied AI systems, particularly methods that can dynamically assess signal trustworthiness without degrading responsiveness to legitimate environmental cues. It motivates research into multi-agent verification schemes and confidence calibration techniques for vision-language models in safety-critical autonomous contexts. The dual-intent framing and evaluation framework enable future work on threat modeling for VLAS in real-world deployment scenarios, spurring development of defenses specific to embodied settings. This research could inform design principles for more trustworthy human-agent interaction where agents remain interpretable about when and why they defer to visual signals, advancing both the security and reliability of autonomous systems operating in complex, potentially adversarial environments.
